explanation: The property states that a control-traffic message (atomic proposition: controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) can only be sent after (not before) the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set components have been queried (atomic propositions: local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set and local_node_queried_duplicate_set, respectively). This means that at any point, if controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi happens, then both local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set and local_node_queried_duplicate_set must have already occurred in the past. In LTL, this safety property is typically enforced by saying: it is never the case that controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs before both queries have happened. Using the 'not ... until ...' pattern with histories, we can specify that: Before both local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set and local_node_queried_duplicate_set have occurred, controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi must not occur. After both have occurred, the action can occur. Formally, this is captured as: !(controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set). But since the property is to hold globally (for every DM_i, or always), we wrap with G (globally): G(!(controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)). This enforces that the sending happens only after both queries. Optionally, if we want the message to be able to occur only once, or to capture per DM_i, the property would be parameterized. But for LTL, we use the atomic propositions as stated.

However, in standard LTL, 'only after' effect is usually expressed as: it is not possible for a control-traffic message to be sent until both queries have occurred ('!(controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)'), and then whenever the control-traffic message is sent, both queries happened earlier. The LTL encoding uses the 'G' operator because the property must always hold, and for the concept of 'only after', we ensure that whenever we see a control-traffic message sent, both the required queries must have happened at some point before. This is expressed using the '!A U B' pattern (where A cannot happen until B does). Specifically, before controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs, both local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set and local_node_queried_duplicate_set must have occurred, so for every time the controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi action occurs, both queries occurred before or simultaneously. explanation:
The property states that the controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi (which means a control-traffic message is sent to DM_i) can only occur after both local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set (queried the MPR Selector Set component) AND local_node_queried_duplicate_set (queried the Duplicate Set component) have already happened. Moreover, this must be true globally (for all times/all messages). Thus, whenever controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi holds, it is necessary that, in the past, both queries must have occurred. In standard LTL, we cannot refer directly to the past, but we can enforce that it is never the case that a control-traffic message is sent before either query happens. This is expressed by saying: 'Globally, if a control-traffic message is sent, then previously both queries have occurred.' In LTL with only future operators, we can rephrase: 'It is never the case (globally, at all times) that a controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs before local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set and local_node_queried_duplicate_set.' Instead, we ensure that in all cases, a controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi is not sent until BOTH queries have happened (which is accomplished with the U operator and conjunction). This is written as: (¬controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set), and globally, any sending of the message is only allowed after. Or, more simply: 'Globally, if controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi happens, then in the past local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set have occurred.' In LTL where only future temporal operators are allowed, the standard idiom is: 'Globally, controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi is only true if (historically) both queries were true' – which in positive LTL can be encoded as: 'Globally, it is always the case that if controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs, then previously both queries must have occurred.'

Therefore, the formula is:
G((!controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) | (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set))

That is, at all times, either the controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi is not sent, or both queries have happened before (or are happening at the same time). Another equivalent way is ensuring that controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi cannot occur before the two queries, expressed as:
G ( ((local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set) -> X F controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) )
This means: 'Globally, after both queries, eventually the control-traffic message can be sent.' But the stricter interpretation, matching the 'must be sent only after...' phrase, is:
G( ((local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set) U controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) | G(!controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) )
But for conciseness and clarity, we use the version with the implication for every occurrence:
G ( controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi -> (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set) )

This means that globally, every time a controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi event occurs, there must have been (in the past or immediately) both queries to MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set. Since LTL is future-oriented, this is typically encoded as: it is never the case that a controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs before both queries have occurred. Alternatively, we can say: globally, if a controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs, then previously local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set and local_node_queried_duplicate_set occurred. But using only future-LTL operators, this yields an encoding: before controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs, both queries must have occurred, which can be written as: globally, it is not the case that controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs until (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set and local_node_queried_duplicate_set) have occurred. This can be expressed using the 'until' operator. Formally: (!controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set), and globally, whenever controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs, both queries have previously happened. Alternatively, a common LTL trick is: G((!controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) | (F(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set))), but this is not precise. The more direct encoding given the constraints is: G((controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) -> (F(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set) & F(local_node_queried_duplicate_set))). But we should enforce order: The message should be sent 'only after' both queries. One canonical encoding in future LTL is: Globally, it is not the case that controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi happens before local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set have happened. That is: (!controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set). To enforce that this always holds: G((!controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)). This guarantees that at every point, the control-traffic message can be sent only after both queries happened.

explanation: The natural language property states that a control-traffic message may only be sent after querying both the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set. We are to formalize the requirement: for every DM_i (globally), 'sending the control-traffic message' is only allowed after BOTH components are queried. This amounts to: globally, if a controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi happens, then in the past there was (already) a query to both local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set AND local_node_queried_duplicate_set. LTL cannot express past directly, but we can express the forward implication: before a controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi, both queries must happen. This is done by requiring that at any point in the future, whenever controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs, then previously (before that, somewhere in the past), both queried events have happened. In forward LTL the closest/safest way is: globally, if controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs, then previously both queries have happened. For causality, a commonly used pattern is: globally, 'if the message is sent, then' in the past 'both queries have happened'. Since LTL is future-oriented and has no past operators, the property is often rewritten as: globally, it is not possible to send the message before querying both components, i.e. 'as long as NOT (both queries), the message cannot be sent'. This is formalized as: 'if not (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set AND local_node_queried_duplicate_set) then not controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi', globally. In LTL: G((!(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)) -> !controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi). Alternatively, we can also write: G((controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) -> (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)).

'Only after' implies that sending the control-traffic message is forbidden until both queries have occurred. This is usually represented in LTL as: it is always the case that if the control-traffic message is sent, then in the past (which is not directly expressible in direct LTL), both queries should have happened. However, the standard translation using future modalities is to express: before the control-traffic message is sent, both queries must occur. A well-accepted pattern is: Globally, if the control-traffic message is sent, then previously (in the path so far), both queries have occurred, but since we use only future-time LTL, we traditionally translate this as: It is forbidden to send the control-traffic message before both queries have occurred. In LTL, this is: as long as not both queries have occurred, the control-traffic message cannot be sent. This can be encoded as: (!(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)) U ((local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set) & X(G(controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi -> (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set))))
But for a simpler, safe variant, we can write:
G((!controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set))
This formula expresses: the control-traffic message cannot be sent until both sets have been queried, for every DM_i.

explanation:
The property states that a control-traffic message (captured by the atomic proposition controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) must only be sent after querying BOTH the MPR Selector Set and the Duplicate Set components (atomic propositions local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set and local_node_queried_duplicate_set). The word 'ONLY AFTER' expresses a precedence: before a controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs, the queries must have happened. In LTL, this is often translated using the globally (G) operator to make the property hold for every point in execution, and an implication from 'sending a control traffic message' to 'having previously queried both sets'.

More precisely, everywhere, if a control-traffic message is sent, then both query actions must have happened previously (or at the same time). This can be encoded by requiring that between any previous time and the occurrence of controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi, neither occurs before the queries:

G(controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi -> (P local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & P local_node_queried_duplicate_set))

However, as standard LTL syntax per the prompt doesn't include the past operator P (previously), we have to encode it using only future operators. The standard method is:

'The controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi does not occur until both queries have happened.'

This is represented by:
G( ((¬local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set | ¬local_node_queried_duplicate_set) U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)) -> ¬controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi )

But more succinctly, we usually require: Controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi can occur only after both queries, and in each run, whenever controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs, both queries occurred in the past. This can be encoded by:

G( (¬local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set | ¬local_node_queried_duplicate_set) -> ¬controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi )

or equivalently,

G( controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi -> ( local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set | X( controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi -> ( local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set ) ) ) )

But the most commonly accepted LTL formula for the 'only after' is:

G( (¬local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set | ¬local_node_queried_duplicate_set) -> ¬controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi )

Alternatively, the more direct way to write in LTL with future operators:

¬controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)

But to make it apply throughout the execution (not just at the beginning), we write:
G ( (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set) || (¬controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)) )

But in most specification, the following is the normal way:

G( controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi -> (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set) )

This says: everywhere, if a controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs, both queries have been done (either at the same time or before).
